summary: I was born in Oklahoma on September 22, 1946, and grew up in a very rural and isolated part of the state. My stepfather worked on the Turner Turnpike and later in restaurants along it. Life there was tough, with segregation and economic hardships, and my family was quite large and complicated. I experienced a lot of challenges growing up, including feeling different because of my family situation and struggling with dyslexia that wasn't diagnosed until much later.

My stepfather's first job was working on the Turner Turnpike, helping to put it across the state of Oklahoma. After that, he worked in one of the restaurants that used to be all along the Turnpike, though I don't know if they still exist today. The Turnpike was a significant part of his early work life and shaped some of our family's experiences in Oklahoma.

I moved to California in 1961 when I was about 17 years old, sent to live with my uncle after some trouble back in Oklahoma. My uncle worked at Laguna Honda Hospital, and living with him introduced me to a very different lifestyle than what I knew. I started working various jobs in San Francisco, including printing services and eventually becoming a longshoreman starting in 1969. California was where I really began to find my footing, despite the challenges I faced.

When I came to California, I lived in San Francisco with my uncle who worked at Laguna Honda Hospital. I worked at a printing service company on Bush Street and later at Copy Service doing layouts for Macy's. San Francisco was where I started my adult working life and eventually got involved with the longshoremen union. It was a big change from Oklahoma and exposed me to new experiences and challenges.

My uncle worked at Laguna Honda Hospital in San Francisco, probably as an orderly or nurse. When I moved to California in 1961, I lived with him and his roommate, and that household was quite lively with drinking and socializing, which was a big change from my life in Oklahoma. Laguna Honda Hospital was part of the environment that introduced me to a new lifestyle in the city.

In my early 20s in San Francisco, I worked at Copy Service as a cameraman doing body layouts for Macy's, especially for their Christmas edition materials. This was after I started out delivering for a printing company called Copycats. Working for Macy's was one of my first steady jobs before I became a longshoreman in 1969.

I got involved with the International Longshore and Warehouse Union in 1969 when they started taking people in. A friend of mine connected me to a longshoreman who said if I paid $300, he could get me on the job. I gave him the money and was eventually picked, though work was very limited for the first several years. The ILWU was a major part of my working life starting in the late 1960s and shaped my career in San Francisco.
